{{Short description|Unreflected, mistaken attributions to and descriptions of social groups}}
{{broader|Stereotype}}
An '''implicit bias''' or '''implicit stereotype''' is the pre-reflective attribution of particular qualities by an individual to a member of some social [[In-group favoritism#Versus out-group negativity|out group]].<ref name="GandB95">{{cite journal |last1=Greenwald |first1=Anthony G. |last2=Banaji |first2=Mahzarin R. |title=Implicit social cognition: Attitudes, self-esteem, and stereotypes |journal=Psychological Review |date=1995 |volume=102 |issue=1 |pages=4–27 |doi=10.1037/0033-295x.102.1.4 |pmid=7878162 |citeseerx=10.1.1.411.2919 |s2cid=8194189 }}</ref> It is also called an unconscious bias that stems from a natural human tendency to divide the social world into groups.<ref>{{cite web
 |title=implicit bias
 |url=https://www.oxfordlearnersdictionaries.com/definition/english/implicit-bias
 |website=Oxford Learner’s Dictionaries
 |publisher=Oxford University Press
 |access-date=2025-12-29
}}</ref>

Implicit stereotypes are thought to be shaped by experience and based on learned associations between particular qualities and social categories, including race and/or gender.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |title=What we can (and can't) infer about implicit bias from debiasing experiments |journal=Synthese |date=February 2021 |volume=198 |issue=2 |pages=1427–1455 |doi=10.1007/s11229-019-02128-6 |s2cid=60441599 }}</ref> Individuals' perceptions and behaviors can be influenced by the implicit stereotypes they hold, even if they are ''sometimes'' unaware they hold such stereotypes.<ref name=pmid24294868>{{cite journal |last1=Hahn |first1=Adam |last2=Judd |first2=Charles M. |last3=Hirsh |first3=Holen K. |last4=Blair |first4=Irene V. |title=Awareness of implicit attitudes |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |date=June 2014 |volume=143 |issue=3 |pages=1369–1392 |doi=10.1037/a0035028 |pmid=24294868 |pmc=4038711 }}</ref> Implicit bias is an aspect of implicit [[social cognition]]: the phenomenon that perceptions, attitudes, and stereotypes can operate prior to conscious intention or endorsement.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gawronski |first1=Bertram |title=Six Lessons for a Cogent Science of Implicit Bias and Its Criticism |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |date=June 10, 2019 |volume=14 |issue=4 |pages=574–595 |doi=10.1177/1745691619826015 |pmid=31181174 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The existence of implicit bias is supported by a variety of scientific articles in psychological literature.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jost |first1=John T. |last2=Rudman |first2=Laurie A. |last3=Blair |first3=Irene V. |last4=Carney |first4=Dana R. |last5=Dasgupta |first5=Nilanjana |last6=Glaser |first6=Jack |last7=Hardin |first7=Curtis D. |title=The existence of implicit bias is beyond reasonable doubt: A refutation of ideological and methodological objections and executive summary of ten studies that no manager should ignore |journal=Research in Organizational Behavior |date=2009 |volume=29 |pages=39–69 |doi=10.1016/j.riob.209.10.001 |doi-broken-date=1 July 2025 }}</ref> 

The term ''implicit stereotype'' was first defined by psychologists [[Mahzarin Banaji]] and [[Anthony Greenwald]] in 1995.<ref name="BanajiGreenwald95">{{Cite journal |last1=Banaji |first1=M. R. |last2=Greenwald |first2=A. G. |year=1995 |title=Implicit gender stereotyping in judgments of fame |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=68 |issue=2 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.68.2.181}}</ref> Implicit stereotypes – unconscious associations held by individuals – can influence behavior even when they contradict consciously endorsed beliefs. This effect is particularly observable in real-world contexts such as judgments of fame and hiring processes.<ref name="BanajiGreenwald95"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bertrand |first=Marianne |last2=Mullainathan |first2=Sendhil |date=September 2004 |title=Are Emily and Greg More Employable Than Lakisha and Jamal? A Field Experiment on Labor Market Discrimination |url=https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/0002828042002561 |journal=American Economic Review |language=en |volume=94 |issue=4 |pages=991–1013 |doi=10.1257/0002828042002561 |issn=0002-8282}}</ref>

Organizations have implemented several evidence-based strategies to reduce implicit bias:

* Blind recruitment processes that remove identifying information
* Standardized evaluation criteria for more objective assessment
* Structured interviews to minimize subjective judgments
* Implicit bias training programs (though their long-term efficacy remains debated)

'''Explicit stereotypes'''<!--boldface per WP:R#PLA-->, by contrast, are consciously endorsed, intentional, and sometimes controllable thoughts and beliefs.<ref>{{cite book|last=Gaertner, Brown|first=Sam, Rupert|title=Blackwell Handbook of Social Psychology: Intergroup Processes|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LNZHf3K4xzMC|access-date=August 11, 2013 |isbn=978-0-470-69270-7|date=April 15, 2008|publisher=John Wiley & Sons }}</ref>

Implicit biases, however, are thought to be the product of associations that were learned through ''past'' experiences.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Del Pinal |first1=Guillermo |last2=Spaulding |first2=Shannon |title=Conceptual centrality and implicit bias |journal=Mind & Language |date=February 2018 |volume=33 |issue=1 |pages=95–111 |doi=10.1111/mila.12166 |hdl=2027.42/142467 |url=https://philpapers.org/rec/GUICCA-4 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Implicit biases can be activated by the environment and operate prior to a person's intentional, conscious endorsement.<ref name=GandB95 />  It has also been proposed that some implicit biases originate early in child development.<ref>{{Cite news |first1=Daniel A. |last1=Yudkin |first2=Jay |last2=Van Bavel |date=December 2016 |title=Opinion {{!}} The Roots of Implicit Bias (Published 2016) |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/12/09/opinion/sunday/the-roots-of-implicit-bias.html |access-date=2025-09-09 |language=en}}</ref> Implicit bias can persist even when an individual rejects the bias explicitly.<ref name="GandB95" />

==Bias, attitude, stereotype and prejudice==
[[Attitude (psychology)|Attitudes]], [[stereotypes]], [[prejudices]], and [[bias]] are all examples of [[Construct (psychology)|psychological constructs]]. Psychological constructs are mental associations that can influence a person's behavior and feelings toward an individual or group. If the person is unaware of these mental associations the stereotypes, prejudices, or bias is said to be implicit.

[[Bias]] is defined as prejudice in favor of or against one thing, person, or group compared with another, usually in a way considered to be unfair. Bias can be seen as the overarching definition of stereotype and prejudice, because it is how we associate traits (usually negative) to a specific group of people. Our "implicit attitudes reflect constant exposure to stereotypical portrayals of members of, and items in, all kinds of different categories: racial groups, professions, women, nationalities, members of the LGBTQ community, disabilities, moral and political values, etc."<ref name=Toribio2018>{{cite journal |last1=Toribio |first1=Josefa |title=Implicit Bias: from social structure to representational format |journal=Theoria |date=March 1, 2018 |volume=33 |issue=1 |page=41 |doi=10.1387/theoria.17751 |doi-access=free |hdl=2445/145839 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

An [[Attitude (psychology)|attitude]] is an evaluative judgment of an object, a person, or a social group.<ref>Crano, W.D., & Prislin, R. (2008). Attitudes and attitude change. New York: CRC Press.</ref> An attitude is held by or characterizes a person. [[Implicit attitudes]] are evaluations that occur without [[Consciousness|conscious]] awareness towards an attitude object or the self.

A [[stereotype]] is the association of a person or a social group with a consistent set of traits. This may include both positive and negative traits and often exaggerate differences between groups. Common types of stereotypes include, racial, cultural, gender and social group (such as those related to profession or age). 

[[Prejudice]] is defined as unfair negative attitude toward a social group or a member of that group.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Gaertner|first1=Samuel L.|author2-link=John Dovidio|last2=Dovidio|first2=John F.|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|volume=8|issue=4|pages=101–105|title=Reducing Prejudice: Combating Intergroup Biases|date=1999|jstor=20182575|doi=10.1111/1467-8721.00024|s2cid=31743564}}</ref> Prejudices can stem from many of the things that people observe in a different social group that include, but are not limited to, gender, sex, race/ethnicity, or religion. This is pertinent to stereotypes because a stereotype can influence the way people feel toward another group, hence prejudice.

==Methods for investigation==
There is a clear challenge in measuring the degree to which someone is biased. There are two different forms of bias: implicit and explicit. The two forms of bias are, however, connected. "Explicit bias encompasses our conscious attitudes which can be measured by self-report, but pose the potential of individuals falsely endorsing more socially desirable attitudes. Although implicit biases have been considered unconscious and involuntary attitudes which lie below the surface of consciousness, some people seem to be aware of their influence on their behavior and cognitive processes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hahn |first1=Adam |last2=Gawronski |first2=Bertram |title=Facing one's implicit biases: From awareness to acknowledgment. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=May 2019 |volume=116 |issue=5 |pages=769–794 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000155 |pmid=30359070 |s2cid=51896790 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/6cqdk/download }}</ref> The [[implicit-association test]] (IAT) is one validated tool used to measure implicit bias. The IAT requires participants to rapidly pair two social groups with either positive or negative attributes."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Maina |first1=Ivy W. |last2=Belton |first2=Tanisha D. |last3=Ginzberg |first3=Sara |last4=Singh |first4=Ajit |last5=Johnson |first5=Tiffani J. |title=A decade of studying implicit racial/ethnic bias in healthcare providers using the implicit association test |journal=Social Science & Medicine |date=February 2018 |volume=199 |pages=219–229 |doi=10.1016/j.socscimed.2017.05.009 |pmid=28532892 }}</ref>

The Implicit Association Test (IAT) was created by Greenwald and Banaji (1995). The IAT measures how strongly people connect social groups such as gender or race with concepts like "good" or "bad". For example someone connecting "men" with career, it might show an implicit bias. Studies such as Nosek, Greenwald, and Banaji (2005) explore how hidden attitudes affect behavior and how people may have automatic biases even when they believe they are being fair.

===Implicit-association test===
The [[implicit-association test]] (IAT) alleges to predict prejudice an individual has toward different social groups. The test claims to do this by capturing the differences in the time it takes respondent to choose between two unassociated but related topics. Respondents are instructed to click one of two computer keys to categorize stimuli into associated categories. When the categories appear consistent to the respondent, the time taken to categorize the stimuli will be less than when the categories seem inconsistent. An implicit association is said to exist when respondents take longer to respond to a category-inconsistent pairing than a category-consistent pairing. The implicit-association test is used in psychology for a wide array of topics. These fields include gender, race, science, career, weight, sexuality, and disability.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Harris |first1=Matthew |last2=Macinko |first2=James |last3=Jimenez |first3=Geronimo |last4=Mullachery |first4=Pricila |title=Measuring the bias against low-income country research: an Implicit Association Test |journal=Globalization and Health |date=December 2017 |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=80 |doi=10.1186/s12992-017-0304-y |pmid=29110668 |pmc=5674740 |doi-access=free }}</ref> While acclaimed and highly influential, the implicit-association test falls short of a strong scientific consensus. Critics of the implicit-association test cite studies that counterintuitively link biased test scores with less discriminatory behavior.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shelton |first1=J. N. |last2=Richeson |first2=J. A. |last3=Salvatore |first3=J. |last4=Trawalter |first4=S. |title=Ironic Effects of Racial Bias During Interracial Interactions |journal=Psychological Science |date=May 1, 2005 |volume=16 |issue=5 |pages=397–402 |doi=10.1111/j.0956-7976.2005.01547.x |pmid=15869700 |s2cid=15621035 }}</ref> Studies have also asserted that the implicit-association test fails to measure unconscious thought.<ref name=pmid24294868/>

Recently new research has found how implicit stereotypes shape behavior even when individuals are explicitly trying to use egalitarian beliefs. Devine et al. (2002) found that automatically activated stereotypes can influence social judgments through unconscious processes. This can include bias such as biased interpretation of behavior. The experiments found that if the participants were exposured to stereotype-related cues it increased the likelihood that participants would evaluate a person in ways that are consistent with the stereotype, even when the participant rejected those beliefs at a conscious level. These findings support dual-process models of social cognition, which propose that implicit and explicit attitudes operate independently and may produce conflicting behavioral tendencies.<ref>Devine, P. G., Plant, E. A., Amodio, D. M., Harmon-Jones, E., & Vance, S. L. (2002). The regulation of explicit and implicit race bias: The role of motivation to respond without prejudice. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82(5), 835–848. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.82.5.835
</ref>

===Go/no-go association task (GNAT)===
The GNAT is similar to the implicit-association test. Although the IAT reveals differential associations of two target concepts (e.g. male-female and weak-strong), the GNAT reveals associations within one concept (for example, whether female is associated more strongly with weak or strong).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nosek |first1=Brian A. |last2=Banaji |first2=Mahzarin R. |title=The Go/No-Go Association Task |journal=Social Cognition |date=December 2001 |volume=19 |issue=6 |pages=625–666 |doi=10.1521/soco.19.6.625.20886 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/4ed36//download }}</ref>

Participants are presented with word pairs among distractors. Participants are instructed to indicate "go" if the words are target pairs, or "no-go" if they are not. For example, participants may be instructed to indicate "go" if the word pairs are female names and words that are related to strength. Then, participants are instructed to indicate "go" if the word pairs are female names and words that are related to weakness. This method relies on signal detection theory; participants' accuracy rates reveal endorsement of the implicit [[stereotype]]. For example, if participants are more accurate for female-weak pairs than for female-strong pairs, this suggests the subject more strongly associates weakness with females than strength.<ref name="Blair01">{{cite journal |last1=Blair |first1=Irene V. |last2=Ma |first2=Jennifer E. |last3=Lenton |first3=Alison P. |title=Imagining stereotypes away: The moderation of implicit stereotypes through mental imagery. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2001 |volume=81 |issue=5 |pages=828–841 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.81.5.828 |pmid=11708560 |citeseerx=10.1.1.555.2509 }}</ref>

===Semantic priming and lexical decision task===
[[Priming (psychology)#Semantic|Semantic priming]] measures the association between two concepts.<ref name="Meyer">{{cite journal |last1=Meyer |first1=David E. |last2=Schvaneveldt |first2=Roger W. |title=Facilitation in recognizing pairs of words: Evidence of a dependence between retrieval operations |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology |date=1971 |volume=90 |issue=2 |pages=227–234 |doi=10.1037/h0031564 |pmid=5134329 |s2cid=36672941 }}</ref>
In a [[lexical decision task]], subjects are presented with pair of words, and asked to indicate whether the pair are words (for example, "butter") or non-words (for example, "tubter"). The theory behind semantic priming is that subjects are quicker to respond to a word if preceded by a word related to it in meaning (e.g. bread-butter vs. bread-dog).<ref name=Meyer /> In other words, the word "bread" primes other words related in meaning, including butter. Psychologists utilize semantic priming to reveal implicit associations between stereotypic-congruent words. For instance, participants may be asked to indicate whether pronouns are male or female. These pronouns are either preceded by professions that are predominantly female ("secretary, nurse"), or male ("mechanic, doctor"). Reaction times reveal strength of association between professions and gender.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Banaji |first1=Mahzarin R. |last2=Hardin |first2=Curtis D. |title=Automatic Stereotyping |journal=Psychological Science |date=May 1996 |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=136–141 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1996.tb00346.x |s2cid=207858486 |url=http://nrs.harvard.edu/urn-3:HUL.InstRepos:34878093 }}</ref>

===Sentence completion===
In a sentence completion task, subjects may be presented with sentences that contain stereotypic black and white names (''Jerome, Adam''), positive and negative stereotypic black behaviors (''easily made the team, blasted loud music in his car'') and counter-stereotypic behaviors (''got a job at Microsoft, refused to dance''). Subjects are asked to add to the end of a sentence in any way that is grammatical, e.g. "Jerome got an A on his test..." could be completed with "because it was easy" (stereotypic-congruent) or "because he studied for months" (stereotypic-incongruent) or "and then he went out to celebrate" (non-explanatory). This task is used to measure stereotypic explanatory bias (SEB): participants have a larger SEB if they give more explanations for stereotype-congruent sentences than stereotype–incongruent sentences, and if they give more stereotypic-congruent explanations.<ref name="Sekaq">{{cite journal |last1=Sekaquaptewa |first1=Denise |last2=Espinoza |first2=Penelope |last3=Thompson |first3=Mischa |last4=Vargas |first4=Patrick |last5=von Hippel |first5=William |title=Stereotypic explanatory bias: Implicit stereotyping as a predictor of discrimination |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=January 2003 |volume=39 |issue=1 |pages=75–82 |doi=10.1016/S0022-1031(02)00512-7 }}</ref>

==Differences between measures==
The Implicit Association Test (IAT), sequential priming, and other implicit bias tests, are mechanisms for determining how susceptible we are to stereotypes. They are widely used in Social Psychology, although measuring response time to a question as a good measure of implicit biases is still up for debate. "Some theorists do question the interpretation of the scores from tests such as the IAT, but the debate is still going on and responses to the criticisms are certainly widespread."<ref name=Toribio2018/>

In [[Qualitative marketing research|qualitative market research]], researchers have described a framework called bias testing to mitigate researcher bias when designing survey questions. It involves empirically testing the survey questions with real-life respondents using interviewer moderated or technology-enabled unmoderated techniques.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last1=Geisen |first1=Emily |url=https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CRGN8RNT |title=Bias testing in market research: A framework to enable inclusive research design |last2=Sha |first2=Mandy |last3=Roper |first3=Farren |year=2024 |isbn=979-8-8629-0278-5 |publication-date=January 3, 2024 |pages=}}</ref>

==Findings==

===Gender bias ===
{{see also|Sexism#Gender stereotypes|Gender role#Gender stereotypes}}

Gender biases are the stereotypical attitudes or prejudices that we have towards specific genders. "The concept of gender also refers to the constantly ongoing social construction of what is considered 'feminine' and 'masculine' and is based on power and sociocultural norms about women and men."<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Hamberg|first=Katrina|date=May 2008|title=Gender bias in medicine|journal=Women's Health|volume=4|issue=3|pages=237–243|doi=10.2217/17455057.4.3.237|pmid=19072473|doi-access=free}}</ref> Gender biases are the ways in which we judge men and women based on their hegemonically feminine and masculine assigned traits.

The category of male has been found to be associated with traits of strength and achievement. Both male and female subjects associate male category members more strongly than female category members with words like ''bold'', ''mighty'', and ''power''.<ref name="RuGrMc">{{cite journal | last1 = Rudman | first1 = L. A. | last2 = Greenwald | first2 = A. G. | last3 = McGhee | first3 = D. E. | year = 2001 | title = Implicit self-concept and evaluative implicit gender stereotypes: Self and ingroup share desirable traits | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 27 | issue = 9| pages = 1164–1178 | doi = 10.1177/0146167201279009 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.43.6589 | s2cid = 13491811 }}</ref> The strength of this association is not predicted by explicit beliefs, such as responses on a gender stereotype questionnaire (for example, one question asked if subjects endorsed the word feminist).<ref name="GandB95" /> In a test to reveal the [[Source-monitoring error#False fame|false fame]] effect, non famous male names are more likely to be falsely identified as famous than non famous female names; this is evidence for an implicit stereotype of male achievement.<ref name="BandG95">{{cite journal | last1 = Banaji | first1 = M. R. | last2 = Greenwald | first2 = A. G. | year = 1995 | title = Implicit gender stereotyping in judgments of fame | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 68 | issue = 2| pages = 181–198 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.68.2.181 | pmid = 7877095 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.74.895 }}</ref> Females are more associated with weakness. This is true for both male and female subjects, but female subjects only show this association when the weak words are positive, such as ''fine, flower'' and ''gentle''; female subjects do not show this pattern when the weak words are negative, such as ''feeble, frail,'' and ''scrawny''.<ref name="RuGrMc" />

Particular professions are implicitly associated with genders. Elementary school teachers are implicitly stereotyped to be female, and engineers are stereotyped to be male.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = White | first1 = M. J. | last2 = White | first2 = G. B. | year = 2006 | title = Implicit and explicit occupational gender stereotypes | journal = Sex Roles | volume = 55 | issue = 3–4| pages = 259–266 | doi = 10.1007/s11199-006-9078-z | s2cid = 143793226 }}</ref>

==== Gender bias in science and engineering ====
[[Implicit-association test]]s reveal an implicit association for male with science and math, and females with arts and language.<ref name="NoBaGr">{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B. A. | last2 = Banaji | first2 = M. R. | last3 = Greenwald | first3 = A. G. | year = 2002 | title = Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 83 | issue = 1| pages = 44–59 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44 | pmid = 12088131 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.463.6120 | s2cid = 204321795 }}</ref> Girls as young as nine years old have been found to hold an implicit male-math stereotype and an implicit preference for language over math.<ref name="steff">{{cite journal | last1 = Steffens | first1 = M. C. | last2 = Jelenec | first2 = P. | last3 = Noack | first3 = P. | year = 2010 | title = On the leaky math pipeline: Comparing implicit math-gender stereotypes and math withdrawal in female and male children and adolescents | journal = Journal of Educational Psychology | volume =  102| issue = 4| pages =  947–963| doi = 10.1037/a0019920 }}</ref> Women have stronger negative associations with math than men do, and the stronger females associate with a female gender identity, the more implicit negativity they have towards math.<ref name=NoBaGr /> For both men and women, the strength of these implicit stereotypes predicts both implicit and explicit math attitudes, belief in one's math ability, and SAT performance.<ref name=NoBaGr /> The strength of these implicit stereotypes in elementary-aged girls predicts academic self-concepts, academic achievement, and enrollment preferences, even more than do explicit measures.<ref name=steff /> Women with a stronger implicit gender-math stereotype were less likely to pursue a math-related career, regardless of their actual math ability or explicit gender-math stereotypes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kiefer |first1=Amy K. |last2=Sekaquaptewa |first2=Denise |title=Implicit Stereotypes, Gender Identification, and Math-Related Outcomes: A Prospective Study of Female College Students |journal=Psychological Science |date=January 2007 |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=13–18 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01841.x |pmid=17362371 |s2cid=30588471 }}</ref> This may be because women with stronger implicit gender-math stereotypes are more at risk for [[stereotype threat]]. Thus, women with strong implicit stereotypes perform much worse on a math test when primed with gender than women who have weak implicit stereotypes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kiefer | first1 = A. K. | last2 = Sekaquaptewa | first2 = D. | year = 2007 | title = Implicit stereotypes and women's math performance: How implicit gender-math stereotypes influence women's susceptibility to stereotype threat | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 43 | issue = 5| pages = 825–832 | doi = 10.1016/j.jesp.2006.08.004 }}</ref>

Though the number of women pursuing and earning degrees in engineering has increased in the last 20 years, women are below men at all degree levels in all fields of engineering.<ref name="nsfreport">{{cite web|title=Women, Minorities, and Persons with Disabilities in Science and Engineering|url=https://www.nsf.gov/statistics/2017/nsf17310/digest/about-this-report/|website=National Science Foundation|access-date=March 31, 2017}}</ref>
These implicit gender stereotypes are robust; in a study of more than 500,000 respondents from 34 nations, more than 70% of individuals held this implicit stereotype.<ref name="Noseketal">{{cite journal | last1 = Nosek | first1 = B. A. | last2 = Smyth | first2 = F. L. | last3 = Sriram | first3 = N. N. | last4 = Lindner | first4 = N. M. | last5 = Devos | first5 = T. | last6 = Ayala | first6 = A. | last7 = Greenwald | first7 = A. G. | year = 2009 | title = National differences in gender–science stereotypes predict national sex differences in science and math achievement | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 106 | issue = 26| pages = 10593–10597 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0809921106 | pmid = 19549876 | pmc = 2705538 | bibcode = 2009PNAS..10610593N | doi-access = free }}</ref> The national strength of the implicit stereotype is related to national sex differences among 8th graders on the International [[TIMSS]], a worldwide math &science standardized achievement exam. This effect is present even after statistically controlling for gender inequality in general.<ref name= Noseketal /> Additionally, for women across cultures, studies have shown individual differences in strength of this implicit stereotype is associated with interest, participation and performance in sciences.<ref name= Noseketal /> Extending to the professional world, implicit biases and subsequent explicit attitudes toward women can "negatively affect the education, hiring, promotion, and retention of women in STEM".<ref name="Jackson, Hillard, Schneider">{{cite journal |last1=Jackson |first1=Sarah M. |last2=Hillard |first2=Amy L. |last3=Schneider |first3=Tamera R. |title=Using implicit bias training to improve attitudes toward women in STEM |journal=Social Psychology of Education |date=September 2014 |volume=17 |issue=3 |pages=419–438 |doi=10.1007/s11218-014-9259-5 |s2cid=53500821 }}</ref>

The effects of such implicit biases can be seen in across multiple studies including:
* Parents rate the math abilities of their daughters lower than parents with sons who perform identically well in school<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Yee |first1=Doris K. |last2=Eccles |first2=Jacquelynne S. |title=Parent perceptions and attributions for children's math achievement |journal=Sex Roles |date=September 1988 |volume=19 |issue=5–6 |pages=317–333 |doi=10.1007/bf00289840 |hdl=2027.42/45585 |s2cid=112300 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
* College faculty are less likely to respond to inquiries about research opportunities if the email appears to be from a woman as opposed to an identical email from a man<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Milkman |first1=Katherine L. |last2=Akinola |first2=Modupe |last3=Chugh |first3=Dolly |title=What happens before? A field experiment exploring how pay and representation differentially shape bias on the pathway into organizations |journal=Journal of Applied Psychology |date=2015 |volume=100 |issue=6 |pages=1678–1712 |doi=10.1037/apl0000022 |pmid=25867167 |url=https://repository.upenn.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1389&context=fnce_papers }}</ref>
* Science faculty are less likely to hire or mentor students they believe are women as opposed to men<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Moss-Racusin |first1=C. A. |last2=Dovidio |first2=J. F. |last3=Brescoll |first3=V. L. |last4=Graham |first4=M. J. |last5=Handelsman |first5=J. |title=Science faculty's subtle gender biases favor male students |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=October 9, 2012 |volume=109 |issue=41 |pages=16474–16479 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1211286109 |pmid=22988126 |pmc=3478626 |bibcode=2012PNAS..10916474M |doi-access=free }}</ref>

An interagency report from the Office of Science and Technology Policy and Office of Personnel Management has investigated systemic barriers including implicit biases that have traditionally inhibited particularly women and underrepresented minorities in [[science, technology, engineering, and mathematics]] (STEM) and makes recommendations for reducing the impact of bias.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Handelsman|first1=Jo|last2=Ward|first2=Wanda|title=Increasing Diversity in the STEM Workforce by Reducing the Impact of Bias|url=https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/blog/2016/12/12/increasing-diversity-stem-workforce-reducing-impact-bias|website=The White House|access-date=March 31, 2017|date=December 12, 2016}}</ref> Research has shown that implicit bias training may improve attitudes towards [[women in STEM]].<ref name="Jackson, Hillard, Schneider"/>

===Racial bias ===
Racial bias can be used synonymously with "stereotyping and prejudice" because "it allows for the inclusion of both positive and negative evaluations related to perceptions of race."<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Noles |first1=Erica |title=What's age got to do with it? Examining how the age of stimulus faces affects children's implicit racial bias |journal=UNLV Theses, Dissertations, Professional Papers, and Capstones |date=May 1, 2014 |publisher=University of Nevada, Las Vegas |id={{ProQuest|1566943023}} |doi=10.34917/5836145 }}</ref> We begin to create racial biases towards other groups of people starting as young as age 3, creating an ingroup and outgroup view on members of various races, usually starting with skin color.

In [[lexical decision task]]s, after subjects are subliminally primed with the word ''BLACK'', they are quicker to react to words consistent with black stereotypes, such as ''athletic, musical, poor'' and ''promiscuous''. When subjects are subliminally primed with ''WHITE'', they are quicker to react to white stereotypes, such as ''intelligent, ambitious, uptight'' and ''greedy''.<ref name="WiJuPa">{{cite journal | last1 = Wittenbrink | first1 = B. | last2 = Judd | first2 = C. M. | last3 = Park | first3 = B. | year = 1997 | title = Evidence for racial prejudice at the implicit level and its relationship with questionnaire measures | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 72 | issue = 2| pages = 262–274 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.72.2.262 | pmid = 9107001 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.462.7827 }}</ref>
These tendencies are sometimes, but not always, associated with explicit stereotypes.<ref name="WiJuPa" /><ref name="GaMc">{{cite journal | last1 = Gaertner | first1 = S. L. | last2 = McLaughlin | first2 = J. P. | year = 1983 | title = Racial stereotypes: Associations and ascriptions of positive and negative characteristics | journal = Social Psychology Quarterly | volume = 46 | issue = 1| pages = 23–30 | doi=10.2307/3033657| jstor = 3033657 }}</ref>

People may also hold an implicit stereotype that associates black category members as violent. People primed with words like ''ghetto, slavery'' and ''jazz'' were more likely to interpret a character in a vignette as hostile.<ref name=devine89 /> However, this finding is controversial; because the character's race was not specified, it is suggested that the procedure primed the race-unspecified concept of hostility, and did not necessarily represent stereotypes.<ref name=WiJuPa />

An implicit stereotype of violent black men may associate black men with weapons. In a video game where subjects were supposed to shoot men with weapons and not shoot men with ordinary objects, subjects were more likely to shoot a black man with an ordinary object than a white man with an ordinary object. This tendency was related to subjects' implicit attitudes toward black people. Similar results were found in a priming task; subjects who saw a black face immediately before either a weapon or an ordinary object more quickly and accurately identified the image as a weapon than when it was preceded by a white face.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Staats|first1=Cheryl|url=https://kirwaninstitute.osu.edu/sites/default/files/2019-06//SOTS-Implicit_Bias.pdf|title=State of the Science: Implicit Bias Review|last2=Patton|first2=Charles|publisher=Kirwan Institute for the Study of Race and Ethnicity at the Ohio State University|year=2013|pages=3, 37–39}}</ref>

Implicit race stereotypes affect behaviors and perceptions. When choosing between pairs of questions to ask a black interviewee, one of which is congruent with racial stereotype, people with a high stereotypic explanatory bias [[Implicit stereotype#Sentence completion|(SEB)]] are more likely to ask the racially congruent stereotype question. In a related study, subjects with a high SEB rated a black individual more negatively in an unstructured laboratory interaction.<ref name=Sekaq />

===In-group and out-group bias===
Group prototypes define social groups through a collection of attributes that define both what representative group members have in common and what distinguishes the ingroup from relevant outgroups.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Hohman|first1= Zachary P.| last2= Gaffney|first2=Amber M.| last3=Hogg|first3=Michael A.| date= September 2017|title= Who Am I If I Am Not like My Group? Self-Uncertainty and Feeling Peripheral in a Group |journal=[[Journal of Experimental Social Psychology]]|volume= 72 |pages= 125–132 |doi= 10.1016/j.jesp.2017.05.002}}</ref> [[In-group favoritism]], sometimes known as in-group–out-group bias, in-group bias, or intergroup bias, is a pattern of favoring members of one's in-group over out-group members. This can be expressed in evaluation of others, in allocation of resources, and in many other ways.<ref name=aronson>Aronson, E., Wilson, T. D., & Akert, R. (2010). ''Social psychology''. 7th ed. Upper Saddle River: Prentice Hall.{{page needed|date=February 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Taylor |first1=Donald M. |last2=Doria |first2=Janet R. |title=Self-Serving and Group-Serving Bias in Attribution |journal=The Journal of Social Psychology |date=April 1981 |volume=113 |issue=2 |pages=201–211 |doi=10.1080/00224545.1981.9924371 }}</ref> Implicit in-group preferences emerge very early in life,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Dunham | first1 = Y. | last2 = Baron | first2 = A. S. | last3 = Banaji | first3 = M. R. | year = 2008 | title = The Development of Implicit Intergroup Cognition | journal = Trends in Cognitive Sciences | volume = 12 | issue = 7| pages = 248–253 | doi=10.1016/j.tics.2008.04.006| pmid = 18555736 | s2cid = 45599721 | url = https://dash.harvard.edu/bitstream/handle/1/2902705/Banaji_ImplicitIntergroup.pdf?sequence=2 }}</ref> even in children as young as six years old. In-group bias wherein people who are 'one of us' (i.e., our ingroup) are favored compared to those in the outgroup, meaning those who differ from ourselves.<ref name="Greenwald, A. G. 2006">{{cite journal | last1 = Greenwald | first1 = A. G. | last2 = Krieger | first2 = L. H. | year = 2006 | title = Implicit Bias: Scientific Foundations | journal = California Law Review | volume = 94 | issue = 4| pages = 945–967 | doi = 10.2307/20439056 | jstor = 20439056 | hdl = 10125/66105 | url = https://scholarship.law.berkeley.edu/californialawreview/vol94/iss4/1 | hdl-access = free }}</ref> Ingroup favoritism is associated with feelings of trust and positive regard for ingroup members and surfaces often on measures of implicit bias.  This categorization (ingroup vs. outgroup) is often automatic and pre-conscious.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Reskin | first1 = B | year = 2000 | title = The Proximate Causes of Employment Discrimination | journal = Contemporary Sociology | volume = 29 | issue = 2| pages = 319–328 | doi = 10.2307/2654387 | jstor = 2654387 | s2cid = 2003571 }}</ref>

The reasons for having in-group and out-group bias could be explained by [[ethnocentrism]], social categorization, oxytocin, etc. A research paper done by [[Carsten De Dreu]] reviewed that oxytocin enables the development of trust, specifically towards individuals with similar characteristics—categorized as 'in-group' members—promoting cooperation with and favoritism towards such individuals.<ref>{{cite journal | last = De Dreu | first = Carsten K.W. | year = 2012 | title = Oxytocin modulates cooperation within and competition between groups: An integrative review and research agenda |journal= Hormones and Behavior |volume= 61 |issue=3 |pages= 419–428 |doi= 10.1016/j.yhbeh.2011.12.009| pmid = 22227278 | s2cid = 140120921 }}</ref> People who report that they have strong needs for simplifying their environments also show more ingroup favoritism.<ref>{{Cite book | last1= Strangor | first1 = Charles | last2= Leary |first2= Scott P. |year=2006 |title= Intergroup beliefs: Investigations from the social side |journal= Advances in Experimental Social Psychology |volume= 38 |pages= 243–281 |doi= 10.1016/S0065-2601(06)38005-7| isbn = 978-0-12-015238-4 }}</ref> The tendency to categorize into ingroups and outgroups and resulting ingroup favoritism is likely a universal aspect of human beings.<ref>{{cite journal | last = Brewer | first = Marilynn B. | year = 2002 | title = The psychology of prejudice: Ingroup love or outgroup hate? |journal= Journal of Social Issues |volume= 55 |issue=3 |pages= 429–444 |doi= 10.1111/0022-4537.00126}}</ref>

We generally tend to hold implicit biases that favor our own ingroup, though research has shown that we can still hold implicit biases against our ingroup.<ref name="Greenwald, A. G. 2006"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reskin |first1=Barbara |year=2005 |title=Unconsciousness Raising |journal=Regional Review, 2005 |volume=14 |issue=3 |pages=32–37 |url=https://www.bostonfed.org/economic/nerr/rr2005/q1/section3a.pdf }}</ref> The most prominent example of negative affect towards an ingroup was recorded in 1939 by [[Kenneth and Mamie Clark]] using their now famous Dolls Test. In this test, African American children were asked to pick their favorite doll from a choice of otherwise identical black and white dolls. A high percentage of these African American children indicated a preference for the white dolls.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Clark| first1 = Kenneth B. | last2=Clark| first2= Mamie P.|year = 1950 | title = Emotional Factors in Racial Identification and Preference in Negro Children |journal= The Journal of Negro Education |volume= 19 |issue=3 |pages= 341–350 |doi= 10.2307/2966491| jstor = 2966491 }}</ref> [[Social identity theory]] and [[Freudian]] theorists explain in-group derogation as the result of a negative [[self-image]], which they believe is then extended to the group.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ma-Kellams |first1=Christine |last2=Spencer-Rodgers |first2=Julie |last3=Peng |first3=Kaiping |title=I Am Against Us? Unpacking Cultural Differences in Ingroup Favoritism via Dialecticism |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=January 2011 |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=15–27 |doi=10.1177/0146167210388193 |pmid=21084525 |s2cid=18906927 }}</ref>

===Other stereotypes===
Research on implicit stereotypes primarily focuses on gender and race. However, other topics, such as age, weight, and profession, have been investigated. 
[[Implicit Association Test|IATs]] have revealed implicit stereotypes reflecting explicit stereotypes about adolescents. The results from these tests claim that adolescents are more likely to be associated with words like ''trendy'' and ''defiant'' than adults.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gross | first1 = E. F. | last2 = Hardin | first2 = C. D. | year = 2007 | title = Implicit and explicit stereotyping of adolescents | journal = Social Justice Research | volume = 20 | issue = 2| pages = 140–160 | doi = 10.1007/s11211-007-0037-9 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.514.3597 | s2cid = 46261770 }}</ref>
In addition, one IAT study revealed that older adults had a higher preference for younger adults compared to older adults; and younger adults had a lower implicit preference for younger adults compared to older adults. The study also found that women and participants with more education had lower implicit preference for younger adults.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chopik |first1=William J. |last2=Giasson |first2=Hannah L. |title=Age Differences in Explicit and Implicit Age Attitudes Across the Life Span |journal=The Gerontologist |date=August 1, 2017 |volume=57 |issue=suppl_2 |pages=S169–S177 |doi=10.1093/geront/gnx058 |pmid=28854609 |pmc=5881761 }}</ref>  IATs have also revealed implicit stereotypes on the relationship between obese individuals and low work performance. Words like ''lazy'' and ''incompetent'' are more associated with images of obese individuals than images of thin ones.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Agerström | first1 = J. | last2 = Rooth | first2 = D. | year = 2011 | title = The role of automatic obesity stereotypes in real hiring discrimination | journal = Journal of Applied Psychology | volume =  96| issue = 4| pages =  790–805| doi = 10.1037/a0021594 | pmid = 21280934 }}</ref> This association is stronger for thin subjects than overweight ones.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Schwartz | first1 = M. B. | last2 = Vartanian | first2 = L. R. | last3 = Nosek | first3 = B. A. | last4 = Brownell | first4 = K. D. | year = 2006 | title = The Influence of One's Own Body Weight on Implicit and Explicit Anti-fat Bias | journal = Obesity | volume = 14 | issue = 3| pages = 440–447 | doi = 10.1038/oby.2006.58 | pmid = 16648615 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
Like explicit stereotypes, implicit stereotypes may contain both positive and negative traits. This can be seen in examples of occupational implicit stereotypes where people perceive preschool teachers as both warm and incompetent, while lawyers are judged as both cold and competent.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Carlsson | first1 = Rickard | last2 = Björklund | first2 = Fredrick | title = Implicit stereotype content: mixed stereotypes can be measured with the implicit association test | journal = Social Psychology | volume = 41 | issue = 4 | pages = 213–222 | doi = 10.1027/1864-9335/a000029 | date = 2010 | s2cid = 144740592 }}</ref>

===Activation of implicit stereotypes===
Implicit stereotypes are activated by environmental and situational factors. These associations develop over the course of a lifetime beginning at a very early age through exposure to direct and indirect messages.  In addition to early life experiences, the media and news programming are often-cited origins of implicit associations.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://kirwaninstitute.osu.edu/research/understanding-implicit-bias/|title=Understanding Implicit Bias|website=kirwaninstitute.osu.edu|language=en-US|access-date=April 6, 2018|archive-date=April 7, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180407022705/http://kirwaninstitute.osu.edu/research/understanding-implicit-bias/}}</ref> In the laboratory, implicit stereotypes are activated by priming. When subjects are primed with dependence by unscrambling words such as dependent, cooperative, and passive, they judge a target female as more dependent. When subjects are primed with aggression with words like aggressive, confident, argumentative, they judge a target male as more aggressive.<ref name="BaHaRo">{{cite journal | last1 = Banaji | first1 = M. R. | last2 = Hardin | first2 = C. | last3 = Rothman | first3 = A. J. | year = 1993 | title = Implicit stereotyping in person judgment | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 65 | issue = 2| pages = 272–281 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.65.2.272 | s2cid = 53051217 | url = http://nrs.harvard.edu/urn-3:HUL.InstRepos:34881476 }}</ref> The fact that females and words such as dependent, cooperative, and passive and males and words like aggressive, confident, argumentative are thought to be associated together suggest an implicit gender stereotype. Stereotypes are also activated by a [[Subliminal stimuli|subliminal]] prime. To exemplify, white subjects exposed to subliminal words that consist of a black stereotype (''ghetto, slavery, jazz'') interpret a target male as more hostile, consistent with the implicit stereotype of hostile black man.<ref name="devine89">{{cite journal | last1 = Devine | first1 = P. G. | year = 1989 | title = Stereotypes and prejudice: Their automatic and controlled components | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 56 | pages = 5–18 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.56.1.5| s2cid = 33975027 }}</ref> However, this finding is controversial because the character's race is not specified. Instead, it is suggested that the procedure primed the race-unspecified concept of hostility, and did not necessarily represent stereotypes.<ref name=WiJuPa /> By getting to know people who differ from oneself on a real, personal level, one can begin to build new associations about the groups those individuals represent and break down existing implicit associations.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.aft.org/sites/default/files/ae_winter2015staats.pdf|author=Cheryl Staats |title=Understanding Implicit Bias|journal=American Educator|date=Winter 2015–2016 |pages=29–43 |access-date=December 3, 2022|language=en}}</ref>

===Malleability of implicit stereotypes===
Implicit stereotypes can, at least temporarily, be reduced or increased. Most methods have been found to reduce implicit bias temporarily, and are largely based on context.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Calanchini |first1=Jimmy |last2=Lai |first2=Calvin K. |last3=Klauer |first3=Karl Christoph |title=Reducing implicit racial preferences: III. A process-level examination of changes in implicit preferences |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=August 27, 2020 |volume=121 |issue=4 |pages=796–818 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000339 |pmid=32852973 |s2cid=221357555 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/vuqa2/download }}</ref> Some evidence suggests that implicit bias can be reduced long-term, but it may require education and consistent effort. Some [[implicit bias training]] techniques designed to counteract implicit bias are stereotype replacement, counter-stereotypic imaging, individuation, perspective taking, and increasing opportunities for contact.<ref name="Devine et al 2012">{{cite journal |last1=Devine |first1=Patricia G. |last2=Forscher |first2=Patrick S. |last3=Austin |first3=Anthony J. |last4=Cox |first4=William T.L. |title=Long-term reduction in implicit race bias: A prejudice habit-breaking intervention |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=November 2012 |volume=48 |issue=6 |pages=1267–1278 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2012.06.003 |pmid=23524616 |pmc=3603687 }}</ref>

Stereotype replacement is when one replaces a stereotypical response with a non-stereotypical response. Counter-stereotypic imagining is when one imagines others in a positive light and replace stereotypes with positive examples.  Individuation is when one focuses on specific details of a certain member of a group to avoid overgeneralizing. Perspective taking is when one takes the perspective of a member of a marginalized group. Increasing opportunities for contact is when one actively seeks out opportunities to engage in interactions with members of marginalized groups.<ref name="Devine et al 2012"/>

'''Self and social motives'''

The activation of implicit stereotypes may be decreased when the individual is motivated to promote a positive self-image, either to oneself or to others in a social setting. There are two parts to this: internal and external motivation. Internal motivation is when an individual wants to be careful of what they say, and external motivation is when an individual has a desire to respond in a politically correct way.<ref name="Burns et al 2017">{{cite journal |last1=Burns |first1=Mason D. |last2=Monteith |first2=Margo J. |last3=Parker |first3=Laura R. |title=Training away bias: The differential effects of counterstereotype training and self-regulation on stereotype activation and application |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=November 2017 |volume=73 |pages=97–110 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2017.06.003 }}</ref>

Positive feedback from a black person decreases stereotypic sentence completion, while negative feedback from a black person increases it.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sinclair | first1 = L. | last2 = Kunda | first2 = Z. | year = 1999 | title = Reactions to a Black professional: Motivated inhibition and activation of conflicting stereotypes | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 77 | issue = 5| pages = 885–904 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.77.5.885 | pmid = 10573871 }}</ref>
Subjects also reveal lesser strength of race stereotypes when they feel others disagree with the stereotypes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Stangor | first1 = C. | last2 = Sechrist | first2 = G. B. | last3 = Jost | first3 = J. T. | year = 2001 | title = Changing racial beliefs by providing consensus information | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 27 | issue = 4| pages = 486–496 | doi = 10.1177/0146167201274009 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.297.266 | s2cid = 144937203 }}</ref> Motivated self-regulation does not immediately reduce implicit bias. It raises awareness of discrepancies when biases stand in the way of personal beliefs.<ref name="Burns et al 2017"/>

'''Promote counterstereotypes'''

Implicit stereotypes can be reduced by exposure to counterstereotypes. Reading biographies of females in leadership roles (such as [[Meg Whitman]], the CEO of eBay) increases females' associations between female names and words like ''leader'', ''determined'', and ''ambitious'' in a gender stereotype [[Implicit Association Test|IAT]].<ref name="DaAs">{{cite journal | last1 = Dasgupta | first1 = N. | last2 = Asgari | first2 = S. | year = 2004 | title = Seeing is believing: Exposure to counterstereotypic women leaders and its effect on the malleability of automatic gender stereotyping | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 40 | issue = 5| pages = 642–658 | doi = 10.1016/j.jesp.2004.02.003 }}</ref> Attending a women's college (where students are presumably more often exposed to women in leadership positions) reduces associations between leadership and males after one year of schooling.<ref name=DaAs /> Merely imagining a strong woman reduces implicit association between females and weakness, and imagining storybook princesses increases the implicit association between females and weakness.<ref name=Blair01 />

'''Focus of attention'''

Diverting a participant's focus of attention can reduce implicit stereotypes. Generally, female primes facilitate reaction time to stereotypical female traits when participants are instructed to indicate whether the prime is animate. When participants instead are instructed to indicate whether a white dot is present on the prime, this diverts their focus of attention from the primes' feminine features. This successfully weakens the strength of the prime and thus weakening the strength of gender stereotypes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Macrae | first1 = C. | last2 = Bodenhausen | first2 = G. V. | last3 = Milne | first3 = A. B. | last4 = Thorn | first4 = T. J. | last5 = Castelli | first5 = L. | year = 1997 | title = On the activation of social stereotypes: The moderating role of processing objectives | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 33 | issue = 5| pages = 471–489 | doi = 10.1006/jesp.1997.1328 }}</ref>

'''Configuration of stimulus cues'''

Whether stereotypes are activated depends on the context. When presented with an image of a Chinese woman, Chinese stereotypes were stronger after seeing her use chopsticks, and female stereotypes were stronger after seeing her put on makeup.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Macrae | first1 = C. | last2 = Bodenhausen | first2 = G. V. | last3 = Milne | first3 = A. B. | year = 1995 | title = The dissection of selection in person perception: Inhibitory processes in social stereotyping | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 69 | issue = 3| pages = 397–407 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.69.3.397 | pmid = 7562387 }}</ref>

'''Characteristics of individual category members'''

Stereotype activation may be stronger for some category members than for others. People express weaker gender stereotypes with unfamiliar than familiar names.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Macrae |first1=C.Neil |last2=Mitchell |first2=Jason P. |last3=Pendry |first3=Louise F. |title=What's in a Forename? Cue Familiarity and Stereotypical Thinking |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=March 2002 |volume=38 |issue=2 |pages=186–193 |doi=10.1006/jesp.2001.1496 |s2cid=34321387 }}</ref> Judgments and gut reactions that go along with implicit biases are based on how familiar something is.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vierkant |first1=Tillmann |last2=Hardt |first2=Rosa |title=Explicit Reasons, Implicit Stereotypes and the Effortful Control of the Mind |journal=Ethical Theory and Moral Practice |date=April 2015 |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=251–265 |doi=10.1007/s10677-015-9573-9 |s2cid=144268523 |url=https://www.pure.ed.ac.uk/ws/files/189177238/VierkantHardtETMP2015ExplicitReasons.pdf }}</ref>

== Developmental origins and real-world consequences ==

Recent research has shown that implicit biases can influence how people perceive children, particularly Black children. A study by Goff et al. (2014) found that Black boys as young as 10 years old were perceived as significantly older and less innocent than White boys of the same age. Participants in the study also judged Black children to be more responsible for their actions in hypothetical scenarios. This phenomenon, known as adultification bias, reflects an implicit stereotype that reduces empathy and increases support for punitive treatment. These biased perceptions are associated with real-world consequences, including disproportionate rates of school discipline and harsher treatment by law enforcement toward Black youth.<ref>Goff, P. A., Jackson, M. C., Di Leone, B. A. L., Culotta, C. M., & DiTomasso, N. A. (2014). The essence of innocence: Consequences of dehumanizing Black children. ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', 106(4), 526–545. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0035663</ref>

Additionally, research has shown that implicit biases can influence real-world decisions even when people consciously endorse fairness. In field audit studies, researchers sent resumes to real job openings, varying the candidates' race via name. Applicants with stereotypically White-sounding names received more callbacks than identical applicants with Black-sounding names, demonstrating subtle racial bias and discrimination.<ref>Bertrand, M., & Mullainathan, S. (2004). Are Emily and Greg more employable than Lakisha and Jamal? American Economic Review, 94(4), 991–1013.</ref> One way to reduce such bias and discrimination is through intergroup contact. In the [[Realistic conflict theory|Robbers Cave experiment]], boys initially divided into competitive groups showed clear prejudice, but working together on shared goals effectively reduced intergroup hostility.<ref>Sherif, M., Harvey, O. J., White, B. J., Hood, W. R., & Sherif, C. W. (1961). Intergroup conflict and cooperation: The Robbers Cave experiment. Norman: University of Oklahoma Book Exchange.</ref> These findings show that while implicit biases often operate outside conscious awareness, structured interventions and positive interactions can reduce their impact on behavior.

==Criticism==
Some social psychology research has indicated that individuating information (giving someone any information about an individual group member other than category information) may eliminate the effects of stereotype bias.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rubinstein |first1=Rachel |last2=Jussim |first2=Lee |date=March 1, 2018 |title=Reliance on individuating information and stereotypes in implicit and explicit person perception |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |volume=75 |pages=54–70 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2017.11.009 |s2cid=148592498 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/8dg25/ }}</ref>

===Meta-analyses===
Researchers from the [[University of Wisconsin–Madison]], [[Harvard University|Harvard]], and the [[University of Virginia]] examined 426 studies over 20 years involving 72,063 participants that used the IAT and other similar tests. They concluded two things:

# The correlation between implicit bias and discriminatory behavior appears weaker than previously thought. 
# There is little evidence that changes in implicit bias correlate with changes in a person's behavior.<ref name="Meta-Analyis Forscher">{{cite journal |last1=Forscher |first1=Patrick S. |last2=Lai |first2=Calvin K. |last3=Axt |first3=Jordan R. |last4=Ebersole |first4=Charles R. |last5=Herman |first5=Michelle |last6=Devine |first6=Patricia G. |last7=Nosek |first7=Brian A. |title=A Meta-Analysis of Procedures to Change Implicit Measures |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2019 |volume=117 |issue=3 |pages=522–559 |doi=10.1037/pspa0000160 |pmid=31192631 |pmc=6687518 }}</ref>

In a 2013 meta-analysis, Hart, Blanton, et al. declared that, despite its frequent misrepresentation as a proxy for the unconscious, "the IAT provides little insight into who will discriminate against whom, and provides no more insight than explicit measures of bias."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Oswald |first1=Frederick |last2=Mitchell |first2=Gregory |last3=Blanton |first3=Hart |last4=Jaccard |first4=James |last5=Tetlock |first5=Philip |title=Predicting Ethnic and Racial Discrimination: A Meta-Analysis of IAT Criterion Studies |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=June 17, 2013 |volume=105 |issue=2 |pages=171–192 |doi=10.1037/a0032734 |pmid=23773046 }}</ref>

===News outlets===
[[Heather Mac Donald]], writing in ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'', noted that:
<blockquote>Few academic ideas have been as eagerly absorbed into public discourse lately as "implicit bias." Embraced by Barack Obama, Hillary Clinton and most of the press, implicit bias has spawned a multimillion-dollar consulting industry, along with a movement to remove the concept of individual agency from the law. Yet its scientific basis is crumbling.</blockquote>

Mac Donald suggests there is still a political and economic drive to use the implicit bias paradigm as a political lever and to profit off entities that want to avoid litigation.<ref name="Wall Street Journal">{{cite news |last1=Donald |first1=Heather Mac |title=The False 'Science' of Implicit Bias |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/the-false-science-of-implicit-bias-1507590908 |access-date=September 2, 2018 |work=The Wall Street Journal |date=October 9, 2017}}</ref>

===Psychometric concerns===
Edouard Machery has argued that "the use of [indirect measures like the implicit association test] is deeply problematic" because the tests do not exhibit the psychometric properties we would expect from measures of "attitudes".<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Machery |first1=Edouard |title=Anomalies in implicit attitudes research |journal=WIREs Cognitive Science |date=2021 |volume=13 |issue=1 |article-number=e1569 |doi=10.1002/wcs.1569 |pmid=34130361 |s2cid=235451166 }}</ref> However, many have already admitted that these indirect tests "assess behavior" rather than attitudes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brownstein |first1=Michael |last2=Madva |first2=Alex |last3=Gawronski |first3=Bertram |title=What do implicit measures measure? |journal=WIREs Cognitive Science |date=2019 |volume=10 |issue=5 |article-number=e1501 |doi=10.1002/wcs.1501 |pmid=31034161 |s2cid=139103999 |url=https://doi.org/org/10.1002/wcs.1501}}</ref> This is an example of how the debate about implicit bias can involve "talking past one another" based on "different expectations of indirect measures", views of (what) implicit bias (is), assumptions about which evidence is relevant, thresholds for scientific significance, psychometric standards, and even norms of science communication.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Byrd |first1=Nick |last2=Thompson |first2=Morgan |title=Testing for implicit bias: Values, psychometrics, and science communication |journal=WIREs Cognitive Science |date=2022 |volume=13 |issue=5 |article-number=e1612 |doi=10.1002/wcs.1612 |pmid=35671040 |s2cid=249479726 }}</ref> So evaluating debates about ''tests'' of implicit bias requires one to pay careful attention to debators' background assumptions and whether (or how well) debators' justify those assumptions.

===Statement by original authors===
Where previously Greenwald and Banaji asserted in their book ''BlindSpot (2013)''.
<blockquote>
Given the relatively small proportion of people who are overtly prejudiced and how clearly it is established that automatic race preference predicts discrimination, it is reasonable to conclude not only that implicit bias is a cause of Black disadvantage but also that it plausibly plays a greater role than does explicit bias.<ref name="Wall Street Journal"/></blockquote>

The evidence presented by their peer researchers led them to concede in correspondence that:
# The IAT does not predict biased behaviour(in laboratory settings)
# It is "problematic to use [the IAT] to classify persons as likely to engage in discrimination".
However, they also stated, "Regardless of inclusion policy, both meta-analyses estimated aggregate correlational effect sizes that were large enough to explain discriminatory impacts that are societally significant either because they can affect many people simultaneously or because they can repeatedly affect single persons."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Greenwald |first1=Anthony G. |last2=Banaji |first2=Mahzarin R. |last3=Nosek |first3=Brian A. |title=Statistically small effects of the Implicit Association Test can have societally large effects |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2015 |volume=108 |issue=4 |pages=553–561 |doi=10.1037/pspa0000016 |pmid=25402677 |s2cid=16000675 |url=https://psyarxiv.com/as2ez/download }}</ref>
